en.extract:
The yellow-crowned flowerpecker (Dicaeum anthonyi) is a species of bird in the family Dicaeidae. It is endemic to Luzon Island in the Philippines. It was formerly conspecific with the Flame-crowned flowerpecker of Mindanao. Its natural habitat is tropical moist montane forest. It is becoming rare due to habitat loss.


Description and taxonomy
Exhibits sexual dimorphism in which males have the eponymous yellow crown and vent while females are much more dull and have uniform olive color and does not the black upperparts
It was formerly conspecific with the Flame-crowned flowerpecker but differs with its yellow crown and vent and yellowish belly.


Ecology and behavior
Feeds on small fruits, the nectar of mistletoes and flowers.Found singly, in pairs or mixed species flocks with other flowerpeckers and small birds. Often observed near fruiting and flowering trees. 
Its breeding ecology is completely undescribed with no documentation of its mating habits, nest, eggs and fledgeling.


Habitat and conservation status
It inhabits tropical moist montane forest from 1,500 to 2,000 meters above sea level.
The International Union for Conservation of Nature assessed this species in 2023 as Least-concern. Prior to this, it was assessed as near threatened with its population being estimated as 10,000 to 19,999 mature individuals. This species is uncommon throughout its range.
The current assessment states that the population is supposedly stable. However, forest loss is a threat especially in its lower altitude limits which are more prone to legal and illegal logging, mining and conversion into farmland and road development. 
It is recommended to investigate potential threats and quantify more precisely the population size of the species. Protect areas of suitable habitat and safeguard against deforestation.


References

The '''yellow-crowned flowerpecker''' ('''''Dicaeum anthonyi''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Dicaeidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Luzon|Luzon Island]] in the [[Philippines]]. It was formerly conspecific with the [[Flame-crowned flowerpecker]] of Mindanao.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Species Updates – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/updates/species-updates/|access-date=2021-05-27|website=www.worldbirdnames.org|language=en-US}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]] is tropical moist [[montane forest]]. It is becoming rare due to [[habitat loss]].

== Description and taxonomy ==
Exhibits [[sexual dimorphism]] in which males have the eponymous yellow crown and vent while females are much more dull and have uniform olive color and does not the black upperparts<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Allen|first=Desmond|title=Birds of the Philippines|publisher=Lynx and Birdlife International Field Guides|year=2020|location=Barcelona|pages=342–343}}</ref>

It was formerly conspecific with the [[Flame-crowned flowerpecker]] but differs with its yellow crown and vent and yellowish belly.

== Ecology and behavior ==
Feeds on small fruits, the nectar of mistletoes and flowers.Found singly, in pairs or mixed species flocks with other flowerpeckers and small birds. Often observed near fruiting and flowering trees. 

Its breeding ecology is completely undescribed with no documentation of its mating habits, nest, eggs and fledgeling.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cheke |first1=Robert |last2=del Hoyo |first2=Josep |last3=Collar |first3=Nigel |last4=Mann |first4=Clive |last5=Bonan |first5=Arnau |last6=Kirwan |first6=Guy M. |last7=Rasmussen |first7=Pamela C. |date=2023 |title=Yellow-crowned Flowerpecker (Dicaeum anthonyi), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/flcflo2/1.0/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.flcflo2.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Habitat and conservation status ==
It inhabits tropical moist [[Montane Forest|montane forest]] from 1,500 to 2,000 meters above sea level.<ref name=":0" />

The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] assessed this species in 2023 as [[Least-concern species|Least-concern.]] Prior to this, it was assessed as [[near threatened]] with its population being estimated as 10,000 to 19,999 mature individuals. This species is uncommon throughout its range.

The current assessment states that the population is supposedly stable. However, [[forest loss]] is a threat especially in its lower altitude limits which are more prone to legal and [[illegal logging]], mining and conversion into farmland and road development. 

It is recommended to investigate potential threats and quantify more precisely the population size of the species. Protect areas of suitable habitat and safeguard against deforestation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=International)|first=BirdLife International (BirdLife|date=2016-10-01|title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Dicaeum anthonyi|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|access-date=2021-09-15|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref><ref>{{Cite report |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/103776016/181041693 |title=Dicaeum anthonyi: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2023: e.T103776016A181041693 |last=IUCN |date=2023-03-03 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2023-1.rlts.t103776016a181041693.en |language=en}}</ref>

zh.extract:
黄冠啄花鸟（学名：Dicaeum anthonyi），是啄花鸟科啄花鸟属的一种，为菲律宾的特有种。该物种的保护状况被评为近危。
黄冠啄花鸟的平均体重约为10.9克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。
